votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq definitions, correctly implementing character case swapping and string mapping.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq definitions, correctly implementing character case swapping and string mapping.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq definitions, using ord and chr for ASCII conversions and string joining for map_string.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_lower_nat checks if a character code is in [97,122], is_upper_nat checks [65,90], swap_ascii converts lowercase to uppercase by subtracting 32 and vice versa by adding 32, map_string applies a function to each character, and flip_case_spec checks if res equals the case-flipped string. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_lower_nat checks if a character code is in [97,122], is_upper_nat checks [65,90], swap_ascii converts lowercase to uppercase by subtracting 32 and vice versa by adding 32, map_string applies a function to each character, and flip_case_spec checks if res equals the result of applying swap_ascii to each character in s. The concrete behavior matches for all inputs.

Both implementations flip ASCII letter case by numeric ranges (65–90, 97–122) and leave other characters unchanged; map over strings identically. No edge cases (e.g., underflow/overflow) arise given the checks, so concrete results match.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it flips case only for ASCII letters (65–90 and 97–122), leaves other chars unchanged, maps over the string, and checks equality. For all ASCII inputs, results match the Coq spec.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it flips case only for ASCII letters using ord/chr with the same numeric ranges, maps over the string elementwise, and checks equality for the spec. For all ASCII inputs, results match Coq’s concrete behavior.
